questions.text: I notice that strong emotions can make me feel physically unsteady.
I find it genuinely easier to give than to receive.
I believe personal gain means little if the people around me are not better off.
I hold the view that maintaining my own well-being is a prerequisite for everything else.
I find that I can mobilize people toward a goal effectively.
I believe that the moral weight of suffering is the single most important consideration when evaluating the ethics of any decision, public or private.
I sense when the real decision-maker in a meeting is not the person with the highest title.
I find that emotional composure during hardship is what allows me to see the full picture rather than just the most painful part.
I believe my emotional steadiness is a genuine trait, not a mask I wear.
I rarely detect when someone is strategically timing a remark.
I feel uncomfortable when I leave things half-finished at the end of the day.
I believe that the moral concern I have for those in pain is not a weakness to be managed but a strength to be honored.
I trust that the group will find its way forward without one person driving.
I often decide what to do only once I'm already in the moment.
I sense the invisible threads of obligation and reciprocity in social groups.
I find that I am usually the one setting the standards for a group.
I break large projects into smaller milestones with specific deadlines.
I believe my emotional intensity is central to who I am.
